title: Buying Supplies — Cash vs On Account
event: Reyes Co. purchases $600 of office supplies. Prepare the entry.

lines.label: asset at cost
lines.why: Supplies are an ASSET when bought — they become expense as they're used.
lines.trap: Not Supplies Expense yet. Expensing happens at the adjusting entry when usage is counted.

lines.label: paid now
lines.why: Paid at purchase — one asset traded for another; total assets unchanged.
lines.trap: An asset-for-asset swap changes NOTHING on the equation's right side — no liability, no equity effect.

lines.label: asset at cost
lines.why: Same asset either way — how you pay doesn't change what you got.
lines.trap: The debit side answers 'what did we get,' never 'how did we pay.'

lines.label: promise to pay
lines.why: A liability until the bill is paid.
lines.trap: Buying on account creates a PAYABLE — no cash line appears in this entry at all.

memorize.body: The debit answers 'what did we get?' The credit answers 'how did we pay (or promise to pay)?'
Supplies at purchase = asset. Supplies Expense waits for the year-end count.

questions.prompt: Reyes buys $600 of supplies on account. How much cash leaves in this entry?

distractors.feedback: 'On account' means pay LATER — the credit goes to Accounts Payable, and cash moves only when the bill is paid.

build.scaffold: Debit what you received (Supplies). Then: paid now → Cash; pay later → Accounts Payable.
